free_text: Neutron capture cross sections of stable Cd isotopes
Five different irradiation sites were used:
--- a thermal column (TC, nominal thermal and
--- two in-core irradiation sites, one in the central
s-1) and a second in the outer core or G-ring (GRICIT,
In some irradiations, rabbit samples were enclosed in a
Cd box (1mm wall thickness) to isolate the epithermal
The gamma rays were observed with high-purity
Ge detectors (nominal volume of 169 cm3, efficiency of
35% compared with NaI at 1332 keV, resolution of 1.68
keV at 1332 keV). Source-to-detector distances for the
cross section measurements were generally 10 to 20 cm,
for which coincidence summing effects are negligible.
The Cd samples were irradiated in the Oregon
State University TRIGA reactor. Irradiations for the
cross section measurements typically lasted 1-3 h in
the ICIT (IC), GRICIT (GR), CLICIT (CL), and thermal
column (TC) sites, and counting began about 2-3 h after
the irradiations. Irradiations in the rabbit (R) were
of duration 5-10 min, and counting began within 20 min
after the irradiations. The measurements with the
non-enriched samples were done prior to the refueling
of the reactor, in which the previous highly enriched
uranium fuel was replaced with low-enriched uranium.
The fuel change resulted in small changes in the net
flux as well as possible changes in the energy
distribution of the neutrons. The measurements with
the enriched samples, which were done after the
refueling, may thus be subject to a neutron spectrum
that is slightly different from that of the
measurements of the non-enriched samples. For
irradiations in the thermal column, where the flux is
nearly Maxwellian, the effective thermal cross
section sigma is essentially identical to sigma0, the
section is proportional to 1/v) . For irradiations in
the core, the effective sigma is larger than sigma0
typically by about 1.5%, which is within the margin
of uncertainty of the present measurement. The
effective resonance integral usually exceeds the
"true" resonance integral I0 by a quantity equal to
about 0.45sigma0, representing the effect of the tail
of the 1/v distribution in the resonance region.
The activities a deduced from measuring the g-
ray intensities were analyzed using the rate equation
for a simple capture and decay process, which gives
is the activity at time t after the end of the
irradiation, R is the reaction rate, and ti is the
irradiation time. The reaction rate can be expressed
as R = N0*(sigma*phith+I*phiepi), where N0 is the
number of target atoms (assumed constant), sigma
represents the effective thermal cross section, I is
the effective resonance integral, and phith and
phiepi are the thermal and epithermal fluxes.
